text: # Retail between local playing field and global shocks

metadata.source_title: Retail between local playing field and global shocks

text: [Sector forecast](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/sectorprognoses/index.html)

metadata.source_title: Retail between local playing field and global shocks

text: [Retail](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/retail/index.html)

metadata.source_title: Retail between local playing field and global shocks

text: [Gerarda Westerhuis](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/gerarda-westerhuis/index.html)

metadata.source_title: Retail between local playing field and global shocks

text: In 2025, we expect a slight volume growth of 1 percent for retail, partly due to a recovery in purchasing power. However, the growth rate remains uncertain due to external factors such as geopolitical instability. Retailers, who are highly dependent on foreign countries, must be flexible and be able to quickly switch between suppliers and transport routes.

metadata.source_title: Retail between local playing field and global shocks

text: In this video, sector economist Gerarda Westerhuis talks in 2 minutes about the growth expectations in retail for 2025 and 2026. See what challenges the retail sector faces and what you can do as an entrepreneur.

metadata.source_title: Retail between local playing field and global shocks

text: [Go to the video](/en/business/insights/sectors-and-trends/sectorprognoses/retail/uncertain-growth-rate-retail-by-geopolitical-instability.html)

metadata.source_title: Retail between local playing field and global shocks

text: Growth varies between the various branches within the retail sector – consisting of retail trade, non-food wholesale and car trade and repair. Drugstores in particular are doing well, while furniture and consumer electronics stores are struggling to grow their sales volumes and turnover. The mutual competition also remains challenging. Large players invest in technology and customer focus, while smaller entrepreneurs must distinguish themselves through specialization, good service and local entrepreneurship.

metadata.source_title: Retail between local playing field and global shocks

text: Our estimates for the retail sector assume an increase in private consumption by 1.1 percent in 2025 and by 0.8 percent in 2026 (ABN AMRO Economic Bureau). But in today's time when consumers have low confidence, they tend to save more. In that scenario, consumption could grow slightly less than expected. Consumers then postpone unnecessary purchases, more often opt for private labels and discounters, and reconsider major expenditure on, for example, a renovation or on more expensive products such as electronics or furniture.

metadata.source_title: Retail between local playing field and global shocks

text: It is estimated that the added value that Dutch retail generates through exports to the United States (US) is more than 300 million euros. This accounts for 7.3 percent of the sector's total exports and almost 1 percent of the total annual turnover of Dutch retail. The latter means that retail dependence on exports to the US is minimal.

metadata.source_title: Retail between local playing field and global shocks

text: Imports from the US are also small compared to many other sectors. If we zoom in specifically on the American products on which the European Union (EU) is considering imposing duties, such as whiskey and clothing, it appears that the Netherlands imported 843 million euros worth of these products from the US in 2023. This is less than 1.6 percent of the total goods imports from the US, the Central Bureau of Statistics (CBS) said.

metadata.source_title: Retail between local playing field and global shocks

text: The relatively low dependence on the US is also reflected in the fact that many retailers are local players, so they are not directly affected by changes in trade policy. For example, as many as 85 percent of retailers indicate that their company does not export. This is evident from the CBS Business Survey of May 2025. Almost all retailers who do export – almost 14 percent – ​​find the import tariffs the most worrying for their export activities in the coming year. And 1.3 percent say they find non-tariff measures such as customs formalities or product requirements worrying.

metadata.source_title: Retail between local playing field and global shocks

text: Non-food wholesaler imports and exports consumer goods such as clothing, electronics and furniture and forms an important connection for smaller companies with foreign countries. However, we do not know exactly how great the import and export dependence of wholesale non-food on the US is. We do know that the total wholesale sector (in jargon Wholesale and trade mediation) exported more than 3.8 billion euros to the US in 2023 and that the turnover of non-food wholesale comprises 20 percent of the total wholesale turnover. An important part of the exports mentioned are re-exports, where goods are imported and then exported in the same or comparable condition without having undergone a significant processing process.

metadata.source_title: Retail between local playing field and global shocks

text: Non-food wholesalers also import a lot from China. This also applies to retailers. While retailers do not have a great dependence on the US, they import relatively a lot from China, an earlier analysis by ABN AMRO showed. Electronics in particular, such as modems and computers, are purchased relatively frequently: together accounting for almost 21 percent of the total imports from China. But clothing (5.5 percent) and furniture (2.5 percent) also often come from China. These products are popular because of their low prices.

metadata.source_title: Retail between local playing field and global shocks

text: [Read also: Geopolitics under the microscope: impact on retail and leisure](/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/retail/geopolitiken-under-the-loep-impact-op-retail-en-leisure.html)

metadata.source_title: Retail between local playing field and global shocks

text: In a scenario in which very high US import duties are again imposed on Chinese products, the fear among European policymakers and entrepreneurs will return that very low-priced Chinese products will flood the European market. Dumping, as it is called, can disrupt market dynamics. Retailers and wholesalers who import items such as toys or clothing from China may benefit from dumping; After all, they can purchase their products at lower prices. At the same time, price dumping can be disadvantageous for retailers and wholesalers who also produce consumer products themselves (vertical integration). As manufacturers, such companies may struggle to remain competitive with low-priced Chinese products.

metadata.source_title: Retail between local playing field and global shocks

text: Although direct dependence on the US appears limited, entrepreneurs should not underestimate the indirect influence of geopolitical tensions. Global trade is affected, creating risks such as disruptions in supply chains and uncertainty about trade agreements and among consumers. Retailers must be flexible and quickly switch between suppliers and transport routes. Real-time insight into the chain helps entrepreneurs identify and solve problems faster. Thinking ahead, distribution of suppliers, scenario analyzes and adjustment of purchasing and pricing strategies can help to limit risks and seize opportunities in an increasingly unpredictable world.

metadata.source_title: Retail between local playing field and global shocks

metadata.source_title: Retail between local playing field and global shocks

text: Today's store is more than just a pantry. It offers customers advice and inspiration and is the place to be in contact with customers. Due to the Ukraine crisis and significant inflation, consumer confidence has fallen sharply and this affects consumer behavior. Retailers are also faced with significant cost increases in the areas of purchasing, rent, personnel costs, Covid recovery payments and energy. Circumstances remain challenging for many retailers even after Covid.
